refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates. High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (a) performance requirements are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (b) baseline is recorded with timestamp, source, and derivation method; (c) history log captures all updates with reason, timestamp, and approver; (d) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline → history is complete and auditable; (e) safety margins are explicitly calculated and documented.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Derive performance targets (cycle time, energy, availability) from worksite mission context inputs
CR-NGHE-005.2: Record derived performance requirements as a traceable baseline with metadata (source, timestamp, derivation method)
CR-NGHE-005.3: Maintain managed history of all performance requirement and calibration updates with full audit trail
CR-NGHE-005.4: Calculate and document explicit safety margins for all performance requirements
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized performance derivation engine (rules-based or ML-driven) that ingests worksite mission context, applies derivation logic, records baseline snapshot, and maintains versioned history in a dedicated database with audit logging
DP-B: Distributed derivation logic embedded in Vehicle and Teleoperation modules, each computing local performance targets and syncing to a shared history log
DP-C: Manual performance requirement entry by Site Manager with post-hoc validation and history tracking via a simple change log
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized performance derivation engine with rules-based logic that ingests worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety policies), applies deterministic derivation algorithms to compute cycle time, energy efficiency, and availability targets, records the derived baseline with full metadata (source, timestamp, derivation method, approver), and maintains a versioned history database with immutable audit trail of all updates and calibration changes.

potential_sources: Coupling to Telemetry interface: derivation engine must ingest real-time or near-real-time worksite context (terrain, payload, environmental conditions) via Telemetry.
Coupling to SafetyAlert interface: safety margin calculations must be informed by active safety policies and alerts.
Coupling to Tasking interface: performance targets must align with tasking directives and mission objectives.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits manageable coupling: it is a consumer of Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking inputs, and a producer of performance baseline records. Coupling is through well-defined interfaces, not through shared state or bidirectional dependencies. Centralization reduces diffusion and improves auditability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context is available in structured form (e.g., terrain classification, payload mass, cycle count targets) via Telemetry or configuration input.
Assumption: Derivation rules are stable and can be codified (not requiring continuous ML retraining in field).
Risk: If worksite context is incomplete or noisy, derived requirements may be inaccurate; mitigation: validation gate and manual override capability.
Risk: Centralized engine becomes a single point of failure; mitigation: redundancy and fallback to cached baseline.
Risk: High safety criticality demands formal verification of derivation logic; mitigation: formal methods or extensive testing of safety-critical paths.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-A is sound. Recommend explicit specification of: (1) derivation algorithm (rules, parameters, safety margin formulas); (2) baseline metadata schema; (3) audit log immutability and retention policy; (4) fallback behavior if derivation fails.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary. It owns the performance derivation engine, baseline management, and history tracking as core system-level capabilities. The engine is logically part of the solution's mission planning and optimization layer, not delegated to Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: NGHE Solution receives worksite mission context (terrain, payload, environmental conditions, operational parameters) from Vehicle and field sensors via Telemetry interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: NGHE Solution receives active safety policies, constraints, and alerts from Safety Services to inform safety margin calculations in performance derivation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: NGHE Solution receives mission objectives and tasking directives to align derived performance targets with operational intent.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outbound: NGHE Solution publishes derived performance baselines and targets to Vehicle and Teleoperation for operational guidance.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: performance derivation is a system-level capability that must be centralized for auditability, traceability, and consistency. Minimal diffusion: all logic and data are owned by NGHE Solution; Vehicle and Teleoperation are consumers only.

Low containment: Vehicle is a physical platform; embedding derivation logic here would scatter system-level policy across hardware and create tight coupling to vehicle-specific parameters. High diffusion risk: multiple vehicles would duplicate logic or require synchronization.

Low containment: Teleoperation is a remote control interface; derivation logic belongs at system level, not in the tele-operator's control layer. High diffusion risk: tele-operator-specific performance targets would diverge from site-wide baselines.

Moderate containment: Safety Services owns safety policies and constraints, which inform performance derivation (e.g., safety margins). However, the full derivation engine (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) is broader than safety scope. Partial ownership: Safety Services provides inputs; NGHE Solution owns the integrated derivation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Performance derivation, baseline recording, and history management are system-level concerns that must be centralized in NGHE Solution to ensure consistency, auditability, and traceability across all operational modes (autonomous, tele-operated, mixed). Centralizing in NGHE Solution avoids diffusion across Vehicle and Teleoperation and ensures a single source of truth for performance baselines.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution module is not yet fully defined or is itself decomposed, the assignment may be premature; mitigation: confirm NGHE Solution has a clear system integration layer.

Risk: Vehicle or Teleoperation may need local performance caching or adaptation; mitigation: NGHE Solution publishes baselines via Telemetry; local modules cache and adapt as needed, but do not own derivation.
Risk: Safety Services may claim ownership of safety margin calculations; mitigation: clarify that Safety Services provides safety constraints as inputs; NGHE Solution integrates them into derivation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Assignment to NGHE Solution is sound. Recommend explicit interface contracts: (1) Telemetry input schema for worksite context; (2) SafetyAlert input schema for safety constraints; (3) Tasking input schema for mission objectives; (4) Telemetry output schema for performance baselines. Recommend also defining the derivation engine as a distinct sub-module within NGHE Solution (e.g., 'Performance Derivation Engine') to clarify ownership and enable future reuse.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (centralized derivation engine) directly addresses all aspects of refined FR: (1) automatic derivation from worksite context, (2) baseline recording with metadata, (3) managed history with audit trail, (4) safety margin calculation. DP-A is traceable to acceptance criteria (documented performance targets, maintained baselines/calibration updates). No decoupling or missing functionality detected.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: DP-A is assigned to NGHE Solution, a single centralized module. No duplication or scattering of derivation logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services.
Interface coupling is well-defined: Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking are inbound; Telemetry is outbound. No hidden dependencies or bidirectional coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score of 95 for NGHE Solution indicates strong fit. The module boundary is clear: NGHE Solution owns the derivation engine and history database; Vehicle and Teleoperation are consumers. Ownership is unambiguous.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) test derivation logic against known worksite scenarios; (2) validate baseline recording and metadata completeness; (3) audit history log for completeness and immutability; (4) trace worksite inputs to derived requirements to baselines to history. All verification activities are testable and measurable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the entire CR-NGHE-005 requirement. No ambiguity about which module is responsible for derivation, recording, or history management. Interfaces are explicit.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the derivation algorithm specification (rules, parameters, safety margin formulas) before detailed design.
Define the baseline metadata schema and audit log immutability policy.
Establish interface contracts for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking inputs.
Plan for formal verification or extensive testing of safety-critical derivation paths (high safety criticality).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 refined FR is not atomic; it decomposes into five sub-FRs (derivation, recording, history, safety margins, traceability). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to design parameters and implementation details. DP-A and IM assignment (NGHE Solution) are sound and pass all gates, but the next level of functional decomposition is necessary to detail the derivation algorithm, baseline schema, history database, and audit mechanisms. Proceeding to the next decomposition level will clarify design and enable detailed implementation planning.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Derive performance targets (cycle time, energy, availability) from worksite mission context inputs
CR-NGHE-005.2: Record derived performance requirements as a traceable baseline with metadata (source, timestamp, derivation method)
CR-NGHE-005.3: Maintain managed history of all performance requirement and calibration updates with full audit trail
CR-NGHE-005.4: Calculate and document explicit safety margins for all performance requirements
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history

assumptions: Worksite mission context is available in structured form via Telemetry (terrain, payload, cycle targets).
Derivation rules are stable and can be codified (not requiring continuous ML retraining).
NGHE Solution module has a system integration layer capable of hosting the derivation engine.
Safety Services provides safety policies and constraints as inputs; NGHE Solution integrates them.
Vehicle and Teleoperation are consumers of performance baselines; they do not own derivation logic.
open_risks: Derivation algorithm accuracy depends on quality and completeness of worksite context inputs; incomplete or noisy data may yield inaccurate targets.
Centralized derivation engine is a single point of failure; redundancy and fallback mechanisms are required.
High safety criticality demands formal verification of derivation logic; extent of formal methods effort is TBD.
Baseline metadata schema and audit log immutability policy must be defined before implementation.
Interface contracts for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking inputs must be formalized.
evidence_needed: Specification of derivation algorithm (rules, parameters, safety margin formulas).
Baseline metadata schema and audit log design.
Interface contracts for Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Tasking inputs.
Formal verification plan or test strategy for safety-critical derivation paths.
Fallback and redundancy strategy for centralized derivation engine.
Traceability matrix linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history.
